summary: Jeff Atwood recounts how his Coding Horror blog suffered catastrophic data loss when a routine hard drive failure corrupted his virtual machine image, which had never been properly backed up. After initially blaming his hosting provider, he accepts full responsibility for not maintaining his own backups. He details the painful recovery process, discovering that text content was easily recoverable from web spider caches, but images were nearly impossible to find. The experience taught him that personal responsibility for backups is non-negotiable, and that recovery planning—not just backup planning—is what matters.
key_points: Taking personal responsibility for your data is essential—relying entirely on hosting providers is gambling with your digital life
Text content posted on the web is effectively immortal due to countless web spiders archiving it, but images are rarely preserved
Recovery planning is more important than backup planning: understanding how you'd actually restore data reveals the hard parts
The community response was overwhelming, with readers and fellow developers contributing cached content and proposing clever recovery schemes
Archive.org is a rare and valuable service attempting an impossible job of preserving web content including images
money_quotes: It is irresponsible of me to leave the fate of my data entirely in someone else's hands, regardless of how reliable they may or may not be.
Apparently, I was gambling that nothing bad would ever happen at the datacenter. Because that's what you're doing when you run without your own backups. Gambling.
I don't think you can actually lose text you post on the web. Not in any meaningful sense; I'm not sure it's possible.
If you can only back up one thing on your public website, it should be the images.
If you aren't personally responsible for your own backups, they are effectively not happening.
Some argue that we shouldn't be talking about 'backups,' but recovery.
Every day is International Backup Awareness Day.
key_insight: True backup responsibility means personally owning your recovery process, not just trusting that someone else's systems will save you when disaster strikes.
